Plaintiff later testified in his deposition that he "ordered supplies one time from Oakland Plumbing Supply . . . [p]robably P.E. O'Hair, I believe, if I pronounced their name right." (Rose Decl., Ex. A [Additional Pages from Plaintiff's Depo., June 7, 2023] at 449:12-17.) He also testified that he "believe[d] it was Oakland Supply at that time" from which "he ordered [asbestos-containing products] at Owens-Illinois." (See id. at 450:6-8.) The Court infers from the evidence in the papers that though Oakland Plumbing Supply and P.E. O'Hair & Co. are separate entities, they were at some point in time informally known as one and the same. Defendant fails to foreclose the relation between the two entities, as well as the possibility that Plaintiff meant P.E. O'Hair & Co. when referencing Oakland Plumbing Supply in his deposition. Defendant fails to show that it asked Plaintiff whether he knew that P.E. O'Hair & Co. and Oakland Plumbing Supply were separate entities or that it asked Plaintiff if he had equated the two entities. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Constr. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81 [stating that "[c]ourts can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered"].) Order signed in open court. Judge: Samuel K. Feng, Clerk: A. Mok. Reporter: Vienna Nguyen, CSR# 13137, email: viennanguyen@gmail.com. (949)295-5737. (503/SKF)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant P.E. Ohair And Co., Erroneously Sued As Westburne Supply Inc.?S Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Sep-07-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Claire C. Weglarz of WOMBLE BOND DICKINSON LLP (424-369-2040) appearing for defendant and moving party P.E. O'Hair & Co.
Kimberly J. Chu of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff and opposing party Garrey Barron.

RULING:
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant P.E. O'Hair & Co.'s Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant. The Court took judicial notice of Exhibit 1 (Plaintiff's Complaint) in the Weglarz Declaration. The Court considered Exhibit 2 (Plaintiff's Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set One), Exhibit 3 (Plaintiff's Responses to Standard Asbestos Case Interrogatories), Exhibit 4 (Plaintiff's Deposition, June 7, 2023), Exhibit 5 (Plaintiff's Deposition, Aug. 30, 2017, Taken in Case No. CGC-15-276397), and Exhibit 6 (Plaintiff's Deposition, Nov. 7, 1996, Taken in Case No. CGC-96-975386) in the Weglarz Declaration. The Court considered Exhibit A (Additional Pages from Plaintiff's Deposition, June 7, 2023) and Exhibit B (Deposition of Defendant's Corporate Representative, Aug. 19, 1992, Taken in Case No. CGC-91-935198) in the Rose Declaration. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that Plaintiff worked with or around an asbestos-containing product supplied by Defendant. (See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) Plaintiff stated in his discovery responses that "[a]t Owens Illinois Glass, [he] performed demolition work and installed a new cooling tower, piping and pumps. [He] also cut and installed new asbestos-containing GARLOCK sheet gaskets." (Weglarz Decl., Ex. 2 [Plaintiff's Responses to Defendant's Special Interrogatories, Set One] at 4:10-14.) He further stated that he "saw P.E. O'HAIR (WESTBURNE SUPPLY, INC.) delivery trucks delivering asbestos-containing gaskets . . . to his employers." (Id. at 4:14-15.) Plaintiff later testified in his deposition that he did not "remember who supplied the products to the Owens-Illinois Glass project that [he] worked on." (Weglarz Decl., Ex. 4 [Plaintiff's Deposition, June 7, 2023] at 447:15-18.) Plaintiff sued Westburne Supply, Inc., which has several alternate entities, including P.E. O'Hair & Co. and Oakland Plumbing Supply. (See Weglarz Decl., Ex. 1 [Plaintiff's Complaint] at 6:14-18.) One of Defendant's corporate representatives testified in another case in this Court that Oakland Plumbing Supply is one of Defendant's subsidiaries. (See Rose Decl., Ex. B [Depo. of Defendant's Corporate Representative, Aug. 19, 1992, Taken in Case No. CGC-91-935198] at 6:2-4, 10:10-11.)

Plaintiff's evidence is sufficient, for purposes of this motion, to show that he has a substantial interest in the action as a whole and that his health is such that preference is necessary to prevent prejudicing his interest in the litigation. (C.C.P. section 36(a); see also Fox v. Superior Court (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 529, 534 [distinguishing the standard required for section 36(a) from the one required for 36(d) and noting that "requiring a showing of what amounts to likely unavailability for trial sets the prejudice standard too high"].) Defendants have not controverted Plaintiff's evidence. Preference under C.C.P. section 36(a) is mandatory, not discretionary, because this action is not a coordinated proceeding. (See Isaak v. Superior Court (2022) 73 Cal.App.5th 792, 798-799.) The Court did not adopt Defendants' conditions. Plaintiff previously filed an asbestos case before this Court. Defendants have not shown that they are unable to obtain necessary information from the previous case or that they cannot prepare accordingly for Plaintiff's deposition in the present case in light of the discovery from the previous case. (See Fox, supra, 21 Cal.App.5th at p. 535, fn. 3 [observing that where a preference motion is made well into the case and the trial date allows "plenty of time for discovery and trial preparation . . . [t]he idea that due process concerns are in play are baseless."].)
